npcs.name: Mother Sable
npcs.title: the Bone-Charm Seller
npcs.hail: Charms against the Wall, dearie — a knuckle of saint, a Kelemvor's-penny…

npcs.name: Sergeant Bram Holloway
npcs.title: Recruiter, the Ashen Watch
npcs.hail: Sign on with the Ashen Watch! Steady pay, hot meals, a cause worth—

npcs.name: Pip
npcs.title: a street child
npcs.hail: *(a small figure drifts a little too close to your purse…)*

variants.text: *(The old charm-seller's milky eyes find you and *sharpen*, the way a merchant's do at a particularly promising customer.)* Ahh. *You.* *(she leans across her stall of knucklebones and knotted thread)* No god's light on you at all, godless one. Which means *you*, of everyone who's wandered past today, actually *need* what old Sable sells. The Wall's already got your name on a list, dearie. Shall we get it crossed off?

*(She clocks the grey balance-sigil at your throat and her sales-smile turns wry.)* A servant of the Judge. *(she snorts)* You don't need a single thing on this table, and we both know it — your soul's got a *reserved seat* downstairs. *(she winks)* But you'll buy a charm anyway. For someone you love who *isn't* as safe as you. Everyone like you does.

*(Her hand darts out and *covers* a tray of charms protectively the instant she sees your mark.)* …Put that away near my stall, would you. *(sourly)* The Bone Lord's sigil curdles my mourning-knots and sours the milk-charms. *(she eyes you)* What's the Lord of the Dead-and-Gone want with a poor woman's trinkets? Browsing, or *cursing*?

*(She takes in your horns — your skin — the things the market's other sellers flinched at, and instead she just nods, businesslike.)* Hells-blood, eh. Or the deep dark. *(she shrugs)* Makes no difference to old Sable — your coin spends the same, and your soul needs warding the same. *(she spreads her hands over the table)* Maybe *more*. What'll it be?

*(A bent old woman presides over a table of knucklebones, knotted thread, and dull copper discs, her voice a practiced sing-song.)* Charms against the Wall, traveler! A knuckle of saint to weigh your soul down sweet, a Kelemvor's-penny for the ferry, a knot of *true* mourning so someone, somewhere, grieves you proper. *(she taps the table)* A copper apiece keeps the nothing off. What'll you have?

nodes.speaker: Mother Sable
nodes.text: *(She waits, gnarled hands folded, the patient stillness of a woman who has out-haggled three generations of this market.)* Well, dearie? Old Sable hasn't got all the daylight left — and neither, between us, have you.

choices.text: [INSIGHT] "These 'saint knuckles' are chicken bones, Mother. That 'Kelemvor's-penny' is a filed-down trade copper. None of this works, does it."
[RELIGION] "A true Kelemvor's-penny is struck on the dark of the moon and never shines. These are mint-bright. You're selling the *idea* of the rite, not the rite."
[FAITHLESS] "Don't sell me the chicken bone. Sell me the one that *actually* works on the godless — if it exists. And don't lie. I'm the Wall's already; I've nothing left to lose by knowing."
[CLERIC] "One grave-tender to another, Mother — what do you sell the people I can't help? The ones with no penny and no god?"
[PERSUASION] "A copper's steep for a chicken bone, Mother. Two charms for one copper, and I'll tell every godless soul on the causeway you're the honest one."
*(let her see, for one blink, the Wall's chill behind your eyes)* "You sell warding against the Wall, Mother. I have *been* to the Wall. I came back. Ask me what actually keeps the nothing off."
"Give me a knot of true mourning. Here's your copper."
"Not today, Mother." *(move on)*

nodes.speaker: Mother Sable
nodes.text: *(The sing-song stops dead. For the first time her milky eyes truly *focus*, and what she sees makes her sit back hard on her stool, one hand drifting to her own knotted charm — the real one, the one she never sells.)* …Oh. *(barely a breath)* Oh, you poor, *cold* thing. You're not lying. *(she studies you the way she's studied forty years of the dying, and finds something she's never seen.)* You went *in*. Into the stone. And something — *(she swallows)* — something pulled you back out with the Wall still *on* you. *(quietly, the merchant entirely gone)* Then you already know what I really sell, don't you. Not bone. Not penny. *(she lifts a plain grey thread)* I sell a soul the *certainty* that when it's gone, one other soul will hold a thread and *remember* it was here. That's the only thing the nothing can't eat: a *witness.* *(she presses the thread into your dead-cold hand, and does not flinch at the cold.)* You came back to be ours, didn't you. The witness. The one who remembers the Faithless when no god will. *(she closes your fingers over it.)* Take it for free, Returned. And when you walk back toward that Wall — and you will — you carry every name this market never got to keep.

nodes.speaker: Mother Sable
nodes.text: *(For a heartbeat her face goes flat and dangerous — and then it *cracks* into the first real smile you've seen in this market, delighted.)* …Ha! *(she slaps the table)* Chicken bones and trade coppers, every one. *(she lowers her voice, conspiratorial, *pleased*)* Forty years I've sold the *comfort*, dearie, not the bone. A frightened soul that *believes* it's warded walks to the Wall calmer, kinder, less alone — and that calm's the only true charm there ever was. *(she presses a knotted thread into your hand, free)* Take that one. It's just thread. But you'll think of being *seen* through, just now, and that's worth more than any saint's knuckle. Go on. No charge for the one who saw me.

nodes.speaker: Mother Sable
nodes.text: *(Her sing-song curdles and she sweeps a protective arm across her wares.)* *Chicken bones.* *(coldly)* You come to a poor woman's table and call her a fraud on a *guess*, with no eye for the rite and no coin in your hand. *(she flaps a hand)* Off with you. The charms work for them as *believe*, and that was never going to be *you*. *(she turns pointedly to the next passerby — and that's the end of Sable's goodwill today.)*

nodes.speaker: Mother Sable
nodes.text: *(The patter drops away entirely. She looks at you a long moment, then reaches *under* the table and sets down a single plain river-stone, smooth and grey.)* …Honest, then. All right. Honest. *(quietly)* There's no charm that keeps the Wall off the godless, dearie. There never was. *(she taps the stone)* But *this* — this is a holding-stone. You give it to someone living, and you tell them: *when I'm gone and unworshipped and they say I was nothing, hold this, and remember I was something to you.* *(she meets your eyes)* That's all any of us get against the nothing. Not a god. A *someone who remembers.* The stone's free. The remembering, you arrange yourself. *(gentler)* It's the only thing on this table that's real, and I only sell it to the ones who'd know the difference.

nodes.speaker: Mother Sable
nodes.text: *(She softens, the merchant giving way to something older and tireder.)* The ones you can't help. *(she nods slowly)* Aye, those are *my* trade, padre — the no-penny, no-god poor your church files under *regrettable.* *(she lifts a knot of grey thread)* I sell them *this*, and a lie that it wards the Wall. *(she shrugs, unashamed)* You'd call it false comfort. I call it the only comfort that's *in stock* for folk your Judge won't seat. *(she presses the knot into your hand)* Take one to the next soul your doctrine fails. Tell them it's blessed. It isn't. But *you* holding it out to them — *that* part's true, and that's the part that works.

nodes.speaker: Mother Sable
nodes.text: *(She cackles, genuinely tickled.)* *Two* for one, and free advertising! *(she counts out two knotted threads)* You drive a thief's bargain, dearie, and you flatter an old woman besides — that's the whole craft, right there, and you've got it. *(she winks)* Two mourning-knots. Tell your godless friends Sable's the honest fraud on the causeway. It's even *true*, mostly. Off you go, and mind the pickpocket by the crates — little Pip's quicker than you'd credit.

nodes.speaker: Mother Sable
nodes.text: *(She's unmoved, the stone wall every market-haggler eventually hits.)* A copper apiece, dearie, and not a chip less. *(she folds her hands)* I've turned away smoother tongues than yours before noon. *(a flicker of warmth)* But you tried honest, and you didn't call me a fraud, so — here. *(she gives you a single knot anyway)* One mourning-knot, full price, and good fortune on the causeway. The Wall's patient. So am I.

nodes.speaker: Mother Sable
nodes.text: *(She takes your copper with the speed of long practice and presses the grey knot into your palm.)* A knot of true mourning. *(she pats your hand)* Now *someone* grieves you proper when the day comes, dearie — even if it's only this thread, and only because you paid for it. *(a crooked smile)* Don't knock it. That's more than half the souls on that Wall ever got. Go safe.

nodes.speaker: Mother Sable
nodes.text: *(She's already looking past you to the next mark.)* Suit yourself, dearie. *(a parting sing-song)* The charms'll be here when the Wall starts feeling closer — and it always does, for everyone, in the end. *(she raises her voice to the crowd)* Charms against the Wall! A copper keeps the nothing off!

variants.text: *(The broad sergeant's recruiting-grin turns genuine the moment he reads how you carry your weight.)* Now *there's* a sword-arm. *(he straightens off his stall)* I've watched a hundred farm-boys try to *look* like they could hold a line today, friend, and you're the first who actually *could.* The Ashen Watch needs you. *(a beat, the grin tightening)* Gods, the Watch *needs* you. Sign on?

*(He marks the way you stand — back to the stall-post, eyes already counting the market's exits — and his pitch dies in his throat, replaced by a soldier's nod to a soldier.)* …You've served. *(quietly)* I can always tell. You're standing a watch right now and you don't even know you're doing it. *(he lowers his voice off the sales-pitch)* So I won't sell you the hot-meals line. You know what a recruiting stall this eager really means. Talk straight with me a minute?

*(His eyes travel up. And up.)* …Well now. *(he recovers his grin)* Built like a postern gate. Friend, the recruits follow a *back* like that without me saying a word — half of soldiering is who the scared lads line up behind, and they'd line up behind *you.* The Ashen Watch could use a wall that walks. Interested?

*(A barrel-chested man in ash-grey tabard hails the crowd from behind a stall hung with the Watch's banner.)* Sign on with the Ashen Watch! Steady pay, hot meals, and a cause worth—! *(his voice catches, just slightly, on the word)* —worth a great deal. *(he recovers, beaming at you)* You, friend! You've the look of someone the city could lean on. A moment of your time?

nodes.speaker: Sergeant Bram Holloway
nodes.text: *(He plants both fists on the stall, the banner of the Ashen Watch sagging behind him in the still air.)* So. What'll it be? The Watch takes all comers with a strong back and a steady nerve — and pays in *actual coin*, which is more than most causes on this causeway manage.

choices.text: [STRENGTH] *(set your elbow on the stall)* "Arm-wrestle for it, Sergeant. I win, you tell me the *real* reason you're a recruiter short of recruits. You win, I'll hear your pitch out."
[INSIGHT] "You don't believe a word of your own pitch, Sergeant. Your voice breaks on 'a cause worth a great deal' every time. Where are you really sending these kids?"
[SOLDIER] "Which front, Sergeant? I've stood a few. The ones that need *this* many bodies *this* fast are never the ones in the recruiting song."
[INTIMIDATION] "Stop. Lying. To children. Pack up the stall, Sergeant, or I'll pack it up for you."

*(speak low, and let him hear the grave in your voice)* "The men you buried on that causeway are standing at your back right now, Sergeant. All three squads. They followed you home. They're not angry — they're *waiting* to see if you stop sending more."
"Where do I sign?"
"Not the soldiering life for me, Sergeant. Good fortune to you."

nodes.speaker: Sergeant Bram Holloway
nodes.text: *(Every drop of colour leaves his broad face. The grin doesn't just fall — it *shatters.* His hand finds the stall-edge and grips it white.)* …How. *(a whisper, ragged)* How could you *know* that. I never — I've told *no one* about the three squads. Not the chaplain, not my wife. *(he turns, slowly, and looks at the empty air behind his own shoulder, where the banner hangs dead-still — and for one terrible second you watch a man almost, almost see them too.)* …Waiting. *(his voice breaks clean in half.)* You're saying they don't *blame* me. They're just — *watching* to see if I— *(he scrubs a fist hard across his eyes.)* I take down the banner. Today. Right now. Gods forgive me, I've sent forty lads up that causeway with a *song*, and the only ones who ever came back were the ones at my *back*, and I knew — I *knew* — *(he starts folding the ash-grey cloth with shaking hands.)* No more. Not one more. *(he looks at you, wrecked and somehow lighter.)* What *are* you, that you carry my dead for me and hand them back gentle? …No. Don't tell me. I don't think I could hold the answer. *(quietly)* Thank you, stranger. They can rest now. So, maybe, can I.

nodes.speaker: Sergeant Bram Holloway
nodes.text: *(He laughs — the first unguarded sound he's made all day — and clasps your hand.)* HA! A soldier's bargain. *(He sets his grip; he's strong, market-stall-strong, but you feel the difference and so does he. His arm goes down slow, then sudden.)* …Gods. *(he flexes his hand, grinning, beaten and glad of it)* All right. All *right.* A deal's a deal. *(the grin fades into something honest and tired)* The real reason. *(low)* It's the Wall, friend. The expedition's to the *Wall* — the church wants bodies to stand the causeway while their Doomguides do something up there they won't name. *(he meets your eyes)* The pay's real. The hot meals are real. The *coming back*… that part I can't promise, and I'm sick to my stomach of not saying so to the farm-boys. You beat the truth out of me fair. Do with it what a soldier does.

nodes.speaker: Sergeant Bram Holloway
nodes.text: *(The grin comes off like a helmet at the end of a long day. He glances at the farm-boys queued at the next stall over and lowers his voice.)* …You see clear. *(quietly)* Aye. I don't believe it. *(he exhales)* The 'cause' is the *Wall*, friend. They're massing bodies to stand the causeway while the church does something up there in the dark they won't put in the recruiting song. Pay's real. Meals are real. The *odds*… *(he shakes his head)* I've buried three squads I signed myself. *(he looks at you)* And every morning I stand this stall and lie sweet to children, because if *I* don't fill the quota, they send a recruiter who *won't* break on the word 'worth.' *(grim)* So now you know. Sign on with your eyes open, or talk me out of the next lad. Either's more than I deserve.

nodes.speaker: Sergeant Bram Holloway
nodes.text: *(The grin snaps back into place, a shade too fast.)* Believe it? Friend, I've *lived* it — twenty years in the ash-grey! *(he booms it for the queue's benefit)* A cause worth a great deal, and don't you let a cynic's squint tell you otherwise! *(but his eyes, just for a second, beg you to drop it)* Now — are you signing, or are you costing me my morning?

nodes.speaker: Sergeant Bram Holloway
nodes.text: *(He doesn't bristle. He *deflates*, like a man who's been waiting for someone to say exactly that.)* …All right. *(quietly, he starts taking down the banner)* All right. You're right. *(he folds the ash-grey cloth with more care than it deserves)* I tell myself someone has to fill the quota, and better me, gentle, than the next one cruel. *(he meets your eyes)* But you're right. There's no gentle way to lie a child onto that causeway. *(he sets the folded banner down)* I'll stand down for the day. Maybe longer. Whatever they do to me for an empty quota, it's less than what I do to *them.* *(roughly)* …Thank you. I think. Now go, before I change my mind and my spine.

nodes.speaker: Sergeant Bram Holloway
nodes.text: *(Twenty years of parade-ground steel come up under the grin, and he does not budge an inch.)* *Mind* yourself. *(flat, hard)* I've held a line against worse than a loud stranger with opinions, and I'll hold this stall against you too. *(he squares up)* Threaten me again at my own post and we'll see which of us the city watch carries off. *(he jerks his chin)* Move along. The Watch doesn't recruit *bullies* — we've a full complement of those already, and they all outrank me.

nodes.speaker: Sergeant Bram Holloway
nodes.text: *(He pushes the muster-roll and a charcoal stick across the stall, and his beam doesn't *quite* reach his eyes.)* Good lad. Good *lass.* Make your mark there. *(as you sign)* …Listen. *(lower, fast, like he can't help it)* Keep your eyes open up on the causeway, hear? Stand at the *back* of the formation, not the front. And if a Doomguide tells you to walk out past the last torch — *(he stops himself)* …ah, never mind an old sergeant's nerves. Welcome to the Ashen Watch. Hot meal's at the barracks. *(he holds your gaze a beat too long)* Come back from it.

nodes.speaker: Sergeant Bram Holloway
nodes.text: *(Something behind his eyes eases — a recruiter quietly relieved to have one *fewer* name on his conscience.)* No? *(he nods, and means it)* No. Fair enough, friend. It's not a life for everyone, and between you and me—  *(he doesn't finish)* —good fortune to you. *(he turns back to the crowd, the grin reassembling)* Sign on with the Ashen Watch! Steady pay, hot meals…! *(softer, just to himself, as you go)* …a cause worth a great deal.

variants.text: *(The small figure sizes you up in a practiced half-second — and, to your surprise, *relaxes*, deciding you're the safe kind.)* …You've got a kind face. *(matter-of-fact, not flattering — appraising)* Kind faces give coppers and don't grab. *(a grubby hand, palm up, hopeful)* Spare one? I'll tell you something true for it. Truer than anything Sable's selling, anyhow.

*(The child flinches back before you've even spoken, reading something in you that makes the small body go tight and ready to bolt.)* —Didn't touch nothing! *(quick, shrill)* Wasn't even *near* your purse, wasn't, you can check— *(already edging toward the gap between the crates)* …you've got *mean* eyes, mister. Missus. Whatever. The kind that don't give coppers. The kind that *grab.*

*(The kid's eyes go wide — not afraid, *fascinated* — taking in your horns, your skin, the strangeness the grown-ups crossed the street to avoid.)* …*Whoa.* *(hushed, delighted)* You're like *me.* The kind grown-ups cross the road from. *(a crooked grin)* That's almost *nice*, that is. Nobody crosses the road from a *little* monster, but a big one? *(the small hand, which had been drifting toward your purse, pauses)* …I won't lift off you. Professional courtesy. Monsters don't rob monsters.

*(You turn, and catch a small grubby hand already halfway to your purse-strings. The child freezes — then produces a smile of pure, practiced innocence.)* …Hi. *(brightly)* Hello. I was just— *(the hand retreats, slow, as if it were never there)* —admiring your *belt.* Lovely belt. Very… belt-shaped. *(a beat)* You didn't see anything, did you, friend? Course you didn't.

nodes.speaker: Pip
nodes.text: *(The child hovers at the gap between two crates — close enough to talk, close enough to *run* — weighing you with the fast, tired arithmetic of someone who has learned that grown-ups are mostly a problem to be managed.)* So? *(wary)* What's it gonna be, then. You the grabbing kind, the preaching kind, or the *boring* kind that just walks off?

choices.text: [PERCEPTION] "Your other hand, Pip. The one behind your back. Open it — slowly — and let's see whose copper that is."
[INSIGHT] "Kids don't pick pockets for fun. You're *fast* and you're *scared* — which means it's not for you. Who are you stealing for, Pip?"
[DECEPTION] "Relax. I'm in the trade too — worked these stalls before you were born. Show me your patch and I'll show you a trick worth two purses."
[GOOD] *(crouch to her level; hold out a copper)* "Here. No tricks, no sermon. Just a copper, and you don't have to tell me anything you don't want to."

*(you've been gentle once already today, and it shows; the kid clocks it the way only the hunted can)* "You don't have to run from me, Pip. I'm the kind that helped someone else in this market not an hour ago. Word travels. Talk to me."

*(you feel the second, guttering little flame before you've decided to)* "There's another light near yours, Pip — small, and low to the ground, and going *out.* Your Wick is sicker than you've let yourself believe. I can feel it from here. Let me help, before there's nothing left to help."
[INTIMIDATION] "Hand. Purse. *Now*, you little gutter-rat, before I call the Watch."
"Keep your hands where I can see them and we'll get along. Carry on, Pip." *(move on)*

nodes.speaker: Pip
nodes.text: *(The child goes utterly, dangerously still — the stillness of a small animal that has just understood the thing in front of it is not another animal at all.)* …How do you— *(her voice comes out tiny)* Nobody knows about Wick. *Nobody.* I keep him *hid*, I keep him *safe*, I— *(her eyes are huge and wet and furious)* —you're not right, are you. You're one of the *cold* ones. The dead-that-walk. *(she doesn't run. She steps *closer*, fists balled, the bravest terrible thing you've seen all day.)* If you can *feel* him going out… *(her whole defiance cracks)* …then it's *true*, the thing I won't let myself— he's not getting *better*, is he. The cough. The grey lips. *(she grabs your dead-cold sleeve with both filthy hands.)* You're a *monster*, and you're the first grown-up who ever told me the *truth*, and I don't — I don't know how to do this without— *(she's crying now, openly, a child again at last.)* Help him. Please. I don't care what you are. *Help my brother.*

nodes.speaker: Pip
nodes.text: *(The small hand comes out from behind the back, and there's your copper, pinched between two filthy fingers. The kid stares at it like it betrayed her.)* …*Traitor coin.* *(she sighs, the whole performance collapsing)* Fine. *Fine.* You got me. *(she doesn't run — that surprises you)* You gonna call the Watch now? *(a tired shrug, far too old for the small face)* Go on, then. The lockup's warmer than the crates, and they feed you Tendays. Worst threat you've got is a *roof.* *(she flips the copper back to you, glum)* Quickest hands on the causeway and I get caught by somebody who actually *looks.* Nobody looks. That's the whole job.

nodes.speaker: Pip
nodes.text: *(She waits, the stolen copper back in your palm, braced for whichever kind of grown-up you turn out to be.)*

choices.text: [INSIGHT] "You didn't run. You're not stealing for yourself — you'd have bolted to protect the haul. Who eats tonight if you don't lift my purse, Pip?"
*(flip the copper back to her)* "Keep it. And the next one's yours too if you tell me straight who you're really lifting for."
"Don't do it again where I can see. Off you go."

nodes.speaker: Pip
nodes.text: *(The child opens both hands wide, palms empty, the very picture of wounded innocence — and your purse, you notice, is still on your belt after all.)* See? *(reproachful)* *Empty.* Both of 'em. You're as bad as the Watch, seeing thieves in every shadow. *(she sniffs)* Maybe I was just standing here. Maybe the crates are a *nice place to stand.* *(but there's the ghost of a grin)* …You looked, though. Most don't. That's something.

nodes.speaker: Pip
nodes.text: *(The practiced patter falls away, and for a second she just looks like what she is — a tired kid by some crates.)* …For Wick. *(quietly)* My brother. He's little. Littler than me, and he don't — he can't *run*, his leg's wrong, so he can't lift, so *I* lift, for both. *(fast, defensive)* I don't take from them as can't spare it! I watched you. You've got *spare.* That's the whole rule — only take from spare. *(she lifts her chin)* It's not *stealing* if they were gonna lose it on Sable's chicken bones anyway. It's just… *moving coppers* to where they're needed more. *(a beat, smaller)* Are you gonna tell the Watch about Wick? They take the little ones to the *workhouse.* Please don't tell about Wick.

nodes.speaker: Pip
nodes.text: *(She watches your face, the whole of her small world balanced on what you do next.)*

choices.text: *(press a fistful of coppers into her hand)* "I won't tell anyone about Wick. Buy him something hot. And come find me if the crates stop being enough."
[PERSUASION] "There's an almshouse by the Kelemvor chapel that doesn't ask questions or split up kin. Mother Cass runs it. Tell her Pip-and-Wick, and tell her a stranger sent you."
"Your secret's safe. But the spare you took stays mine. Look after your brother, Pip."

nodes.speaker: Pip
nodes.text: *(Her eyes narrow, then *gleam* — a fellow professional, supposedly.)* …You're in the trade? *(she circles you once, appraising)* Huh. You've got the *patter*, I'll give you that. *(she sticks out a grubby hand to shake — and you feel her other hand ghost toward your purse even as she does it, testing you)* Partners, then. Show me this two-purse trick, *partner.* *(the grin is all teeth)* …'Course, if you were *lying* about being in the trade, that'd make you just another mark playing dress-up. And I don't show my patch to *marks.* So which is it?

nodes.speaker: Pip
nodes.text: *(The shutters come down. Whatever you reached for, you missed, and she's not giving it away for free.)* …Dunno what you mean. *(flat, practiced)* I'm just a kid by some crates. *(she pockets her hands)* You're the *preaching* kind, I reckon. Ask a lot of questions, give nothing, walk off feeling clever. *(she edges back)* Go on, then. Go feel clever somewhere else. The crates were here first.

nodes.speaker: Pip
nodes.text: *(She takes a step back, the offer landing wrong on a kid who's heard a hundred grown-up promises.)* …An almshouse. *(suspicious)* That's what they *all* say, mister. 'A nice place, doesn't split you up.' And then it's the workhouse and they put Wick on one cart and me on another and that's the *last I ever—* *(she stops, breathing hard)* …No. No, I look after Wick. *Me.* Not your Mother Cass. *(she's already backing into the crate-gap)* …but. *(a flicker)* …if it gets bad. Maybe. I'll *remember* the name. That's all. Don't follow me.

nodes.speaker: Pip
nodes.text: *(She stares at the coppers in her hand, then at you, with the open, undone astonishment of a child who genuinely did not know this was a thing grown-ups did.)* …Oh. *(very quietly)* You — you just *gave* it. No trick. No sermon. *(she closes her fist around the coins like they might escape)* People don't. People *don't.* *(she scrubs roughly at one eye with the back of her wrist and pretends she didn't)* …Wick's gonna eat *hot* tonight. With *gravy.* *(a fierce, wobbling grin)* I'm not gonna lift off you. Not ever. You're on the list — the *good* list, the one that's just got you on it now. *(she's already darting toward the crates, then turns back)* …If you're ever stuck on the causeway, you find a kid and you say *'Pip says we're square.'* They'll help you. We *all* help the good-list ones. *(and she's gone, quick as a sparrow, richer by a few coppers and the rarer thing besides.)*

nodes.speaker: Pip
nodes.text: *(There's no skill-check to this one. The word 'Watch' does what a closed fist couldn't — the child goes white and bolts, a streak of grubby terror vanishing between the crates before the threat's even finished leaving your mouth.)* *(Somewhere in the gap between the barrels, a small voice, shaking:)* —wasn't even gonna take much— *(and then nothing. The coppers Pip didn't get stay in your purse. The thing you could have learned about why a child works this market alone goes with her.)*

nodes.speaker: Pip
nodes.text: *(The child relaxes a fraction — a grown-up who isn't a problem after all.)* …Right. *(a nod, almost respectful)* The boring kind. *(but not unkindly)* That's the best kind, mostly. *(she settles back against the crates, watchful)* Mind how you go. And mind your purse near the fountain — there's *worse* than me works this square, and they don't give the belt-admiring speech first. *(a quick grin)* That one's free. Professional courtesy.
